concepts.title: Simple Titration
concepts.conceptBody:
Simple Titration

A known volume of the solution of unknown concentration is taken in a flask and required reagents are added to it. The solution of known concentration is added from the burette in the solution of unknown concentration till the latter reacts completely. This process is called titration. At the end point (equivalence point) the equivalents or milli-equivalents of the two reacting substances are equal.

$\text{Volume of solution (A) }={{\text{V}}_{\text{A}}}\text{ litres }$

$\text{Normality of solution }(\text{A})\text{ }={{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}$

$\text{ Equivalents of substance }(\text{A})\text{ }={{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}{{\text{V}}_{\text{A}}}\text{ }$

$\text{ Similarly, equivalents of substance }(\text{B})\text{ }={{\text{N}}_{\text{B}}}{{\text{V}}_{\text{B}}}$

At the equivalence point (end point) the equivalents (not the moles) of the two substances equal.

${{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}{{\text{V}}_{\text{A}}}(\text{ litre })={{\text{N}}_{\text{B}}}\quad {{\text{V}}_{\text{B}}}(\text{ litre })$

Example 21

$40\text{mlN}/10\text{HCl}$ and $60\text{mlN}/20\text{KOH}$ are mixed together. Calculate the normality of the acid or base left. What is the normality of the salt formed in the solution?

Solution. 

Milli equivalents of HCl = N $\text{V}(\text{ml})=\frac{1\times 40}{10}=4$

Milli equivalents of KOH = N \[\text{V}(\text{ml})=\frac{1\times 60}{20}=3\]

One milli equivalent of an acid neutralizes one milli equivalent of a base

Milli equivalent of $\text{HCl}$ left $=4-3=1$

Total volume of the solution $=40+60=100\text{ml}$

Milli equivalent of HCl = N V(ml)

1 = N 100

Normality $(\text{N})$ of $\text{HCl}$ left in solution $=0.01$

Salt formed = Milli equivalent of acid or base neutralized

Milli equivalents of the salt formed = N V(ml)

3 = N 100

Normality (N) of salt formed = 0.03

concepts.title: Double Titration
concepts.conceptBody:
Double titration

If an aqueous solution contains a mixture of any two of the three $\text{NaOH},\text{NaHC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ and $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ and it has to be titrated against an acid $\text{HCl}$ or ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$, it will require two indicators to determine the strength of the bases present. The two indicators used are phenophthalein and methyl orange.

Method 

A given volume of the aqueous solution of the bases is taken and phenophthalein indicator is added to it. This solution is titrated with an acid of known normality to the end point the volume of the acid used to noted. This end point is called first end point. Now in the same solution methyl orange is added and gain titrated with an acid of known strength to the end point. It is called second end point. The volume of acid, used in the second end point is also noted. The normality of the bases present is then calculated.

Principle: In the presence of phenophthalein indicator

(i) $\text{NaOH}$ is completely neutralized by the acid

(ii) Only half of the milliequivalents of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ present are titrated as the phenophthalein will show the colour change when only $\text{NaHC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ (weak base) and neutral species are left in the reaction mixture. The following reactions take place,

$\text{NaOH}+\text{HCl}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{NaCl}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{ N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{HCl}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{NaHC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{NaCl}$

weak base

Since phenolphthalein is a weak organic acid, and it changes its colour in weakly basic medium $\left( \text{NaHC}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)$, so as soon as the $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ is converted to $\text{NaHC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$, phenophthalein shows the colour change indicating the completion of the reaction.

In the presence of methyl orange, all the basic structures, left in the mixture will be neutralized by acid and methyl orange will show the colour change when the medium is weakly acidic $\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}} \right.$ i.e. $\left. {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)$

1. Titration of the solution containing both NaOH and $\mathbf{N}{{\mathbf{a}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{C}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{3}}}$: A given volume of the aqueous solution is titrated with an acid of known normality using phenophthalein indicator. Suppose ‘a’ milli equivalents of acid are used in the first end point then, milli equivalents of $\text{NaOH}+1/2$ milli equivalent of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

= milli equivalent of acid = a … (1)

Now in the same already titrated solution methyl orange indicator is added and again titrated to the end point. Suppose ‘b’ milli equivalents of the acid are used at the second end point.

½ milli equivalents of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}=$milli equivalents of acid = b … (2)

From equation (1) and (2)

Milli equivalents of acid used by $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}=2~\text{b}$

$=\text{ milli equivalents of N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

Milli equivalents of acid used by $\text{NaOH}=\text{a}-\text{b}$

$=\text{ milli equivalent of NaOH}$

Knowing the milli equivalents of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ or $\text{NaOH}$ and the volume of the solution titrated their normality can be calculated.

Example 22

$\text{NaOH}$ and $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ are dissolved in $200\text{ml}$ aqueous solution. In the presence of phenophthalein indicator, $17.5\text{ml}$ of $0.1~\text{NHCl}$ are used to titrate this solution. Now methyl orange is added in the same solution titrated and it requires $2.5\text{ml}$ of the same HCl. Calculate the normality of $\text{NaOH}$ and $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ and their mass present in the solution.

Solution. 

Milli equivalents (a) of $\text{HCl}$ used in the presence of phenolphathalein indicator

$=\text{N}\quad \text{V}(\text{ml})$

$=0.1\quad 17.5=1.75$

$1.75\text{ (a) = milli eq}\text{. of NaOH}+1/2\text{ milli eq}\text{. of N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}.$ … (1)

Milli eq. (b) of$\text{HCl}$ used in the presence of methyl orange indicator

$=\text{N}\quad \text{V}(\text{ml})$

$=0.1\quad 2.5=0.250.25$

$0.25\,\,(b)=1/2\text{ milli equivalents of N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ … (2)

For $\mathbf{N}{{\mathbf{a}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{C}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{3}}}$ Solution. From equation (2)

Milli eq. of acid used by $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}=2~\text{b}$

$=2\quad 0.25=0.5$

Volume of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ solution $=200\text{ml}$

Suppose, Normality of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}=\text{N}$

Milli equivalents of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}=\text{N}\quad \text{V}(\text{ml})=200~\text{N}$

Putting equivalents of acid and $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ equal

$200~\text{N}=0.5$

Or (Normality of$\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$solution)$\text{N}=\frac{1}{400}$

$\text{ Mass of N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}=\text{N}\quad \text{E}\quad \text{V (litre) }$

($\text{E}$ for $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}=53$ ) $=\frac{1}{400}\times 53\times 0.2$

$=\mathbf{0}.\mathbf{0265}\text{ gram }$

For $\text{NaOH}$ Solution. From equation (1) and (2)

$\text{Milli eq}\text{. acid used by NaOH}$$=\text{a}-\text{b}=1.75-0.25$

$=1.50\text{ }$

$\text{Volume of NaOH solution }=200\text{ml}$

$\text{Suppose, Normality of NaOH solution }=\text{N }$

$\text{Milli eq}\text{. of NaOH }=\text{N}\quad \text{V}(\text{ml})=200~\text{N}$

Putting the milli eq. of $\text{NaOH}$ and acid used equal

$200~\text{N }=1.5~$

$(Normalityof$NaOH$solution)$ $\text{N}=\frac{1.5}{200}$

$\text{Mass of NaOH }=\text{N}\quad \text{E}\quad \text{V (litres)}$

$\text{ }=\frac{1.5}{200}\times 40\times 0.2$

$(\text{E}$ for $\text{NaOH}=40)=\mathbf{0}.\mathbf{06g}$

concepts.title: Back Titration
concepts.conceptBody:
Back titration

Let us consider that we have an impure solid substance ‘$\text{Z}$’ weighing ‘$\text{w}$’ $g$ and we are required to calculate the percentage purity of ‘$\text{Z}$’ in the sample. We are also provided with two solutions 
‘$\text{X}$’ and ‘$\text{Y}$’, where the concentration of ‘$\text{Y}$’ is known $\left( {{\text{N}}_{1}} \right)$ and that of ‘$\text{X}$’ is unknown. For the back titration to work, following conditions are to be satisfied.

(a) Compounds ‘$\text{X}$’, ‘$\text{Y}$’ and ‘$\text{Z}$’ should be such that ‘$\text{X}$’ and ‘$\text{Y}$’ reacts with each other.

(b) ‘$\text{X}$’ and pure ‘$\text{Z}$’ also reacts with each other but impurity present in ‘$\text{Z}$’ does not react with ‘$\text{Z}$’.

$\text{Z}+\text{X (excess) }\to \text{ Product }1.$

Remaining $(\text{X})+\text{Y}\to $ Product 2

Note: Product 1 should not react with $Y$ 

Milli equivalent of $\text{Y}={{\text{N}}_{2}}~{{\text{V}}_{2}}$

Where ${{\text{N}}_{2}}$ and ${{\text{V}}_{2}}(\text{ml})$ is the normality and volume of $\text{Y}$

Initial milli equivalent of $\text{X}={{\text{N}}_{1}}~{{\text{V}}_{1}}$

Where ${{\text{N}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{V}}_{1}}(\text{ml})$ is the normality and volume of $\text{X}$

Remaining milli equivalents of $\text{X}$ after reacting with $\text{Y}={{\text{N}}_{1}}~{{\text{V}}_{1}}-{{\text{N}}_{2}}~{{\text{V}}_{2}}$

Remaining milli equivalents of $\text{X}=$ milli equivalents of $\text{Z}$

${{\text{N}}_{1}}~{{\text{V}}_{1}}-{{\text{N}}_{2}}~{{\text{V}}_{2}}=\frac{\text{a}100}{\text{ Equivalent weight }}$

Where ‘$a$’ is the weight of pure $\text{Z}$ which is reacted.

$a=\frac{\text{ Molecular weight }\left( {{N}_{1}}{{V}_{1}}-{{N}_{2}}{{V}_{2}} \right)}{n-\text{ factor }}$

$\therefore $ $\text{Percentage purity of Z}Z=\frac{\left( {{N}_{1}}{{V}_{1}}-{{N}_{1}}{{V}_{2}} \right)}{n-\text{ factor }}\times \frac{\text{ Molar Mass of }Z}{W}\times 100$

Example 24

$20~\text{g}$ of sample of $\text{Ba}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}$ is dissolved in $10\text{ml}$ of $0.5~\text{NHClsolution}.\text{TheexcessofHCl}$ was titrated with $0.2~\text{NNaOH}$. The volume of $\text{NaOH}$ used was $10\text{cc}$. Calculate the percentage of $\text{Ba}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}$ in the sample.

Solution. 

Milli eq. of $\text{HCl}$ initially $=10\quad 0.5=5$

Milli eq. of $\text{NaOH}$ consumed = Milli eq. of $\text{HCl}$ in excess

$=10\quad 0.2=2$

$\therefore $ Millie q. of HCl consumed = Millie q. of $\text{Ba}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}$

$=5-2=3$

$\therefore $ $\text{eq}\text{. of Ba}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}=3/1000=3\quad {{10}^{-3}}$

$\text{ Mass of Ba}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}=3\quad {{10}^{-3}}$  $(171/2)=0.2565~\text{g}$

$\%\text{ of Ba}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}=\left( \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 0.2565/20) & 100=1.28\% \\ \end{array} \right.$

concepts.title: Iodimetry and Iodometry
concepts.conceptBody:
Iodimetry and Iodometry

i. Iodimetry

This titration involves free iodine. Such direct estimation of iodine is called iodimetry. This involves the titration of iodine solution with known sodium thiosulphate solution, whose normality is $\text{N}$. Let the volume of sodium thiosulphate used by V litre.

$\underset{(\text{n}=2)}{\mathop{{{\text{I}}_{2}}}}\,+\underset{(\text{n}=1)}{\mathop{2\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}~{{\text{S}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}}}\,\to 2\text{NaI}+\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}~{{\text{S}}_{4}}{{\text{O}}_{6}}$

Equivalents of ${{\text{I}}_{2}}=$ Equivalents of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}~{{\text{S}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ used $=\text{N}\quad \text{V}$

Moles of ${{\text{I}}_{2}}=\frac{\text{N}\times \text{V}}{2}$

Mass of free ${{\text{I}}_{2}}$ in the solution $=\left[ \frac{\text{N}\times \text{V}}{2}\times 254 \right]\text{g}$

(ii) IODOMETRY

This is an indirect method of estimation of iodine. An oxidising agent is made to react with excess of solid KI. The oxidising agent oxidises ${{\text{I}}^{-}}$to ${{\text{I}}_{2}}$. This liberated iodine is then made to react with $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}~{{\text{S}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ solution of normality $\text{N}$. Let the volume of thiosulphate solution required be $\text{V}$ litre.

Oxidising Agent$(\text{A})+\text{KI}\to {{\text{I}}_{2}}\xrightarrow[{}]{2\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}~{{\text{S}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}}2\text{NaI}+\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}~{{\text{S}}_{4}}{{\text{O}}_{6}}$

reduced form of oxidizing agent

Equivalents of ‘A’ = equivalents of \[{{I}_{2}}\] = Equivalents of Na 2 S 2 O 3 used = N V

Equivalents of \[{{I}_{2}}\] liberated from KI = N V

Equivalents of ‘A’ = N V

Let the $\text{n}$-factor of ‘ $\text{A}$ ‘ in its reaction with $\text{KI}$ be $\text{x}$, then

Mass of ‘A’ consumed $\text{=}\frac{\text{N}\times \text{V}}{\text{x}}\times {{\text{M}}_{\text{A}}}\text{ (where }{{\text{M}}_{\text{A}}}\text{ is the molar mass of A ) }$

Example 25

A $1.1~\text{g}$ sample of copper are is dissolved and $\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}$ (aq.) is treated with $\text{KI}{{\text{I}}_{2}}$ liberated required $12.12~\text{mL}$ of $0.1\text{MN}{{\text{a}}_{2}}~{{\text{S}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ solution for nitration. What is % of $\text{Cu}$ in the ore?

Equivalent of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}~{{\text{S}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ = equivalent of ${{\text{I}}_{2}}$

= equivalent of $\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}$

$\text{w}(\text{pure }(\text{Cu}))=\frac{\text{NEV}}{1000}$

$=\frac{0.1\times 63.5\times 12.12}{1000}$

$=0.077~\text{g}$

This is present in 1.1 g copper ore.

% of copper = 7.0%

This is present in 1.1 g copper ore.

$%$ of copper $=7.0%$

examples.title: Which of the following solutions will exactly oxidize $25 \mathrm{~mL}$ of an acid solution of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ iron () oxalate:

Oxalic acid, $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$, reacts with paramagnet ion according to the balanced equation $5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(\mathrm{aq})+$ $2 \mathrm{MnO}_{4^{-}}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+10 \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+8 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$. The volume in $\mathrm{mL}$ of $0.0162 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{KMnO}_4$ solution required to react with $25.0 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.022 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ solution is :

The
can detect the endpoint of a titration involving the reaction of $\mathrm{MnO}_4$ - to Mn2+.
Due to which of the following reasons can potassium permanganate not be prepared as a primary standard?
Excess $\mathrm{KI}$ reacts with $\mathrm{CuSO}_4$ solution, and then $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ solution is added to it. Which of the statements is incorrect for this reaction?
Dichromate ion reduces to __________ ion in an acidified solution of potassium dichromate (VI).
